coredata.dc:title: Citric acid functionalized neomycin carbon dots for cytotoxicity and sensing application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Khobrekar, Pritesh P.
Zalmi, Geeta A.
Raiturker, Antara P.
Jadhav, Ratan W.
Ganguly, Anasuya
D'Costa, Avelyno
Bugde, Sandesh T.
Bhosale, Sheshanath V.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials have been applied in various fields. Currently, functionalized carbon nanomaterial with practical tuning and surface functionalization is a tremendously used method. Herein, neomycin-based fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized via hydrothermal method using neomycin and citric acid as a source for selective sensing of Fe3+ metal ions. The as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs exhibited blue fluorescence, successfully characterized and utilized for sensing various metal ions. The carbon dots showed excellent sensing properties towards Fe3+ metal ions and optical properties were characterized by a fluorescence spectrofluorometer. In addition, pH and biocompatibility studies were investigated for the Neo-CA-CQDs. The average size of the Neo-CA-CQDs was 3–5.7 nm. The quantum yield of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs was 29.3 %. The limit of detection for Fe3+ sensing was determined to be 6.09 μM. The sensing of Fe3+ ions was also confirmed through visual detection, as a significant color change of the CQDs was observed. Additionally, a competitive study was conducted to verify the selectivity of the neomycin citric acid-mediated CQDs towards Fe3+. The in vitro cytotoxicity study was performed on a mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) and Human cervical cancer cell line (HeLa).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial
Carbon quantum dot
Cytotoxicity
Fluorescence
Sensing

Effect of ionic strength and masking agent
Fluorescence response of Neo-CA-CQDs towards Fe3+ion
UV–Vis absorption response of Neo-CA-CQDs to Fe3+ion
Sensing mechanism of Neo-CA-CQDs for Fe3+ metal ion
Quenching efficiency and competitive binding of Neo-CA-CQDs for Fe3+ ion
Stern-Volmer plot and limit of detection

We acknowledge DST-FIST,UGC and SCS, Goa University for the instrumentation facility. We acknowledge IIT Bombay SAIF for providing HR-TEM facility, IIT Kanpur for XPS facility and IISc Bangalore for time resolved fluorescence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a), b), and c) describes HR-TEM images at various scale bars (10 to 2 nm), respectively, and d) the diameter distribution of as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs.
XPS spectra of Neo-CA-CQD (a) Survey, (b-e) represents C1s, O1s, N 1s, S 2p(3/2) XPS spectra.

a) UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra of Neo-CA-CQDs in distilled water (excitation wavelength = 370 nm). (b) Fluorescence spectra in DDW at different excitation wavelengths and (c) fluorescence spectra of Neo-CA-CQDs in different solvents (λex = 370 nm) d) P-XRD of Neo-CA-CQDs e) FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs and f) FT-IR spectra of neomycin.
Solutions of Neo-CA-CDs in DDW with and without addition of Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K +, Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ba2+,Sn2+ and Fe2+, Fe3+metal ions (1mM) under 365 nm UV light.

Emission spectra of Neo-CA-CQDs upon excitation at 370 nm wavelength. (a) Emission of Neo-CA-CQDs with various metal ions in DDW and (b) Fluorescence titration of Neo-CA-CQDs with the incremental addition of Fe3+ ion in DDW.
a) UV–Vis spectra of as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. b) .Sensing of Fe3+ ions using Neo-CA-CQDs at different pH.
The plausible sensing mechanism of Neo-CA-CQDs of neomycin with citric acid by hydrothermal method.

a) Fluorescence quenching of Neo-CA-CQDs by Fe3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, and Fe2+,metal ions b) Pictogram of fluorescence spectra of Neo-CA-CQDs exposed to various metal ions and the mixture of Neo-CA-CQDs and Fe3+ in DDW.
(a) plot for determining the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ions. (b) Stern−Volmer plot for Neo-CA-CQDs with Fe3+ions.
A plot of life time decay for Neo-CA-CQDs.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of Neo-CA-CQDs.
Mechanistic pathway for the synthesis of Neo-CA-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Citric acid functionalized neomycin carbon dots for cytotoxicity and sensing application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials have been applied in various fields. Currently, functionalized carbon nanomaterial with practical tuning and surface functionalization is a tremendously used method. Herein, neomycin-based fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized via hydrothermal method using neomycin and citric acid as a source for selective sensing of Fe3+ metal ions. The as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs exhibited blue fluorescence, successfully characterized and utilized for sensing various metal ions. The carbon dots showed excellent sensing properties towards Fe3+ metal ions and optical properties were characterized by a fluorescence spectrofluorometer. In addition, pH and biocompatibility studies were investigated for the Neo-CA-CQDs. The average size of the Neo-CA-CQDs was 3–5.7 nm. The quantum yield of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs was 29.3 %. The limit of detection for Fe3+ sensing was determined to be 6.09 μM. The sensing of Fe3+ ions was also confirmed through visual detection, as a significant color change of the CQDs was observed. Additionally, a competitive study was conducted to verify the selectivity of the neomycin citric acid-mediated CQDs towards Fe3+. The in vitro cytotoxicity study was performed on a mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) and Human cervical cancer cell line (HeLa).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data supporting the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) belong to the family of carbon nanomaterials, which was first obtained during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 by Xu et al. [1–3]. They are generally oxygenous carbon nanoparticles having a size of less than 10 nm, excellent luminescence, high photostability, and facile surface modification. In recent years, extensive efforts have been made to develop advanced carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, and carbon dots due to their unique mechanical, thermal, electronic, and optical properties [4,5]. Among them, carbon quantum dots are a new member of photoluminescent nanomaterials. The luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots allow applications in various fields, such as bioimaging, sensing, photocatalysis, analytical chemistry, solar cells, supercapacitors and optoelectronic devices [6]. Carbon quantum dots are biocompatible and have low toxicity, suggesting they are more suitable for biomedical applications than metal-based quantum dots [7,8]. There are metal doped carbon dots such as doping the carbon dots with gadolinium (Gd) which makes promising component to be employed for magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging technique [9]. Many researchers also worked on various green approaches for synthesis of carbon dot material that has excellent application in the field of supercapacitors [10–12].

Well-designed and modified CQDs are synthesized as a sensing system for ions, amino acids, and pH. Further increasing the efficiency of sensing multifunctional carbon dots with surface modification shows excellent properties. There are multiple CQDs preparation methods, which are divided into two major categories: the bottom-up method and the top-down method. The top-down methods include electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, and chemical and arc discharge. In contrast, the bottom-up approach utilizes methods such as thermal decomposition, plasma treatment, hydrothermal method, and microwave treatment [13–15]. The bottom-up synthesis approach is highly attractive due to its easy tuning properties and ease of handling [16]. Amongst all aforementioned techniques, a hydrothermal method is employed frequently for the synthesis process due to its ease of operation, and this method is simple [17].

Various fluorescent materials have been developed for the selective and sensitive detection of metal ions, anions, and neutral molecules. However, one advantage of the carbon dots over other fluorescent materials is their solubility. It is observed that most of the synthesized carbon dots are soluble in water and hence found to be more applicative over other fluorescent materials. A wide range of fluorescent carbon dots have been synthesized by functionalizing the carbon material with organic compounds and biomolecules, making it a strong candidate for sensing applications [18,19].

Recently, our group synthesized the neomycin antibiotics-based fluorescent material for sensing Fe3+ metal ions. In addition, another fluorescent carbon dot was synthesized with tartaric acid and functionalized tryptophan, which was used to sense Hg2+ and Fe3+ metal ions [20]. However, another research article represents the crafting of acetone CQDs used for dual response to pH and Fe3+ ion sensing [21,22]. The Fe3+ metal ion is one of the essential elements that plays an excellent role in oxygen transport, acts as a cofactor in various enzyme-catalyzed reactions, and is an essential component in cellular metabolism. High levels or deficiencies of Fe3+ metal ions may lead to severe abnormalities such as anemia, heart disease, diabetes, or even may lead to arthritis. For this reason, qualitative and quantitative analysis of Fe3+ metal ions in different mediums is necessary. Many highly sophisticated techniques can be employed for the detection of metal ions, such as voltammetry, spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and atomic absorption spectroscopy, as well as one can employ the fluorescence method for determining the Fe3+ metal ion as this method is considered as the easiest and simple approach over another instrumental method [23,24].

In this work, we modified the surface with citric acid using a facile and green hydrothermal approach to synthesize the Neo-CA-CQDs using neomycin and citric acid as a carbon and nitrogen source (Scheme 1 ). However, to further study its functional and optical properties with enhanced sensing properties, we designed and functionalized the neomycin co-structure with citric acid. The as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs exhibit good water solubility with excellent optical and fluorescence properties. Moreover, as prepared Neo-CA-CQDs have been developed as a selective and sensitive probe for detecting Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution. In addition to sensing applications, carbon dots can be highly utilized for biological toxicity and cellular imaging studies [25–28]. Further, an MTT assay was performed to study the toxicity of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs.

All metal ions Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+,Hg2+, Mn2+, K+ and Sn2+were obtained from chloride salt. Zn2+ and Al3+ions were obtained from nitrate salt. Mg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Ba2+ions obtained from sulfate source. All the metal salts were purchased from TCI Chemicals, Hyderabad, India, and used directly without further purification. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Sigma Aldrich make. Double distilled water (DDW) was used during all the experiments. A Shimadzu UV−Vis-1800 spectrophotometer was used to measure UV−Vis absorption spectra, and emission spectra were recorded on the Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectro fluorophotometer. All the sample solutions were kept at room temperature during the spectrophotometric measurements.

The Neo-CA-CQDs were prepared through the hydrothermal method using citric acid and neomycin sulfate as carbon sources and nitrogen and sulfur sources. Firstly, 0.1 g of neomycin sulfate and 0.2 g of citric acid were added to 10 mL of distilled water. After that, the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave bomb and was kept in the oven for 6 h at 200 °C. After cooling it to room temperature, a dark-brownish color solution was obtained. This dark-brown color solution was centrifuged for 20 min at 25,000 rpm, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to get pure Neo-CA-CQDs. Using the freeze-drying technique, the CQDs were dried for 72 h and stored at 5 °C for later use (Scheme 1). During the reaction, the neomycin sulfate and citric acid undergo bond breaking, and ring-opening takes place when it is heated at high temperature; further, the reaction with water forms acid. In addition, the carbonization of the carbon moiety occurs in the next step, followed by the aromatization of sp3 carbon to form Neo-CA-CQDs (Scheme 2 ).

To investigate its size, shape, and surface morphology, The as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs were characterized using HR-TEM. The HR-TEM images of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs reveal that the synthesized carbon dots spherical nanoparticles were well dispersed and separated. The HR-TEM micrograph and the particle size distribution histogram are displayed in Fig. 1 . The sizes of Neo-CA-CQDs were mainly distributed within a narrow range of 3 to 5.7 nm. The calculated interplanar spacing for the Neo-CA-CQDs was 0.35 nm which confirmed form HR-TEM. Figure S12.

FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to further characterize the surface functionalization of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 e, the broad peak at 3158 cm-1 corresponds to NH and OH stretching vibration, while the peak around 1716 cm-1 indicates the presence of carbonyl (C = O) group on the surface of the Neo-CA-CQDs. These results confirm that the Neo-CA-CQDs are functionalized with oxygen-containing groups such as carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl. The absorption peak at 1571 cm-1 is mainly due to the C = N stretching vibrations. The peak at 1403 cm-1 appears from the stretching vibration of C = C in Neo-CA-CQDs. The broad peak at 1213 cm-1 originates from the CO, CN stretching vibration. These results indicate the presence of C, N, and O elements on the surface of Neo-CA-CQDs.

The Neo-CA-CQDs was further characterized by PXRD, which was recorded using powder XRD – Rigaku Smartlab X-ray diffractometer using X-ray source CuKa radiation. The sample was placed on a silicon low background sample holder and subjected to the theta to 2θ scan range from 10 to 60°. As represented in plot Fig. 3 d, the synthesized carbon dots depicts the intense peak at θ = 21.30°, which confirms that the Neo-CA-CQDs was successfully synthesized as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs exhibited sharp diffraction pattern in the 2θ range of 20°−40°suggesting ordered carbons in Neo-CA-CQDs.

Further to investigate the composition and valence state elements of synthesized Neo-CA-CQDs XPS measurements were performed. The XPS measurements were performed to investigate the composition and valence state of elements of the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs. The XPS spectrum of the Neo-CA-CQDs depicts the peaks at 168.59, 228.28, 285.48, 284.32, 400.32, and 531.91 eV corresponding to binding energy of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The chemical compositions of Neo-CA-CQDs were C (64.19 %), N (9.35 %), O (24.88 %), S (1.57 %) based on the XPS data (Inset in Fig. 2
a), disclosing higher sulfur and nitrogen contents. The High resolution XPS spectra of C1s Fig. 2 b showed three peaks with binding energies of about 288.28, 285.48, and 284.32 eV Here, the peak at 288.28 eV corresponds to (C = O/C = N), the peak located at 285.48 eV reflects the C = C bonding structure and the peak at 284.32 eV are mainly attributed to CC/CH bonds. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s showed one peaks centred at 400.32 eV Fig. 2 d. The XPS spectra of O1s region showed one peak at 531.91 eV which corresponds to COH Fig. 2 c. The S 2p spectrum of Neo-CA-CQDs is composed of one peak centred at 168.59 eV (S 2P3/2) eV Fig. 2 e suggested existence of sulphur in the carbon nanostructure which is well interpreted with FT-IR results. Thus, the above analysis indicates that the as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs synthesized have functional groups like ‒COOH, –OH, and –NH2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic light scattering experiment was performed for synthesized carbon dots for studying size distribution profile of carbon dots suspended in solution. The average size obtained was 138.85 nm, this can be due to agglomeration of the carbon dots in solution. However the obtained zeta potential value of the carbon dot was −15.75 mV. The negative value suggest that the surface of the carbon dots is functionalized with negative charge moieties. Figure S13

The UV–Vis absorption and emission behavior of Neo-CA-CQDs has been investigated, as depicted in Fig. 3
a. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Neo-CA-CQDs exhibits an intense absorption band at 205 nm, mainly due to the π–π* transitions of C = C and C = N groups. In the fluorescence spectra, the intense emission band was observed at 450 nm (λex = 370 nm) in DDW.

Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission studies were performed for Neo-CA-CQDs upon excitation from 370 nm wavelength with increasing wavelength from 250 nm to 450 nm. During this study, it can be noted that the fluorescence emission intensity showed a red shift for various reasons, such as the functionalization of the surface and the size, which are essential factors. The observed highest emission intensity at 450 nm (Fig. 3 b). Moreover, the quantum yield (Φ) of as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs was measured in DDW and found to be 29.3 % using quinine sulfate as the reference. This attractive property of functionalized Neo-CA-CQDs leads to excellent sensing applications. The emission property of carbon dots was well studied in different solvent systems such as double distilled water (DDW), acetone, ethanol, acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethylformamide (DMF), and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO). As represented in the solvent study, it is observed that water and DMSO exhibited the highest emission intensity compared to other solvents at 450 nm. (Fig. 3 c). Due to the highest fluorescent intensity in water, water can be considered a green solvent. In addition to this, the solubility of the synthesized carbon dots is very high in water; therefore, for further analysis, water is marked as an excellent solvent over other solvents. The significant difference in its emission property is due to the solvatochromic effect on carbon dots [29].

For plausible structural characterization, a tentative interpretation of a structural unit of Neo-CA-CQDs, as reported in Fig. 4 . 13CNMR was performed. It is concluded that 13C NMR spectra have a very general peak assignment. In the 13C NMR spectrum Fig. 4, the regions found are: 30–80 ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups), δ 110–130 ppm (for C = C aromatic or sp2 carbons), and δ 175–190 ppm (for C = O carbons).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength and masking agent
ce:section.ce:para: The ionic strength of Neo-CA-CQDs was examined by using NaCl solution with varying concentrations from 0.05M to 0.25 M The emission intensity was recorded for Neo-CA-CQDs by adding 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 M of NaCl solution. However, there was no change in fluorescence intensity with the addition of NaCl at varying concentrations. However, there was a significant increase in fluorescent intensity as the concentration of NaCl increased from 0.5 to 1M This ionic strength modification indicates that there is an electrostatic reaction and that it is unlikely to undergo any salt interference [30,31]. From the reported literature, there is a kind of aggregate formation due to electrostatic interaction resulting in enhancement in fluorescence. Figure S1(a) [32] This indicates that the synthesized carbon dots has excellent NaCl resistance and is well-suited for biochemistry applications [33].

In addition, the masking agent's effect was studied by using ethylenediamine (EDTA) as a masking agent with a concentration range from 0.01 to 0.05M, and the fluorescence emission intensity was recorded with an addition of 0.01, 0.02,0.03,0.04, and 0.05 M EDTA to the stock solution, containing Neo-CA-CQDs. However, this study observed that the masking agent did not affect the synthesized citric acid-modified neomycin carbon dots (Figure S2 (a)).

In recent years, CQD-based sensors have been extensively used as efficient sensors of metal ions and biomolecules compared to other materials such as molecular probes. Colorimetric and fluorimetric detection have been proven to be very easy and attractive tools for evaluating chemosensors selectivity towards particular metal ions. The excellent photoluminescent properties of Neo-CA-CQDs, such as strong emission and good quantum yield, are mainly ascribed to the quantum confinement effect (QCE) and edge effect. The unique luminescent properties of Neo-CA-CQDs make them effective sensors for the detection of metal ions, proteins, and macromolecules based on fluorescence turn-on/turn-off mechanisms. In this study, we used Neo-CA-CQDs to identify Fe3+, which has been investigated by colorimetric and fluorometric experiments.

The stock solution of Neo-CA-CQDs (1 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving in 10 mL DDW. The series of metal ions stock solution (1mM) such as FeCl3, CuCl2, CaCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, Zn(NO3)2, CdCl2, Mg2SO4, PbSO4, Al(NO3)3, HgCl2, BaSO4, MnCl2, CaCl2, KCl and SnCl2 was prepared. All the metal ions were added to the Neo-CA-CQDs series of solutions. After adding all metal ions, the vials were observed under UV light illumination at 365nm. Fig. 5 . It was observed that under 365 nm UV light, the blue fluorescence of the Neo-CA-CQDs gets quenched after the addition of Fe3+metal ions. In contrast, other metal ions did not show fluorescence quenching. The visual detection results suggested that Neo-CA-CQDs act as good fluorescent material for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, we confirmed the sensing characteristics of Neo-CA-CQDs using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. To draw out the selectivity study, we measured competitive experiments for Neo-CA-CQDs toward Fe3+metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Neo-CA-CQDs towards Fe3+ion
ce:section.ce:para: The metal sensing properties of Neo-CA-CQDs were studied by fluorescence spectroscopy. After visual detection for various actions, the solution was subjected to a fluorescence spectrophotometer instrument, and it was observed that with the addition of metal cations Pb2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, and Sn3+ to the solutions containing Neo-CA-CQDs. When the molecule was excited at 370 nm with different cations in series, it is observed in the plot that in the presence of all cations, the synthesized carbon dots was highly selective towards the Fe3+ metal ion. From the plot, it can be concluded that when Fe3+ was added, there was a complete decrease in fluorescence intensity or quenching of fluorescence emission intensity at 450nm. This indicates that the Neo-CA-CQDs is an excellent candidate for sensing application (Fig. 6 ). However, to further confirm its sensitivity towards Fe3+ metal ion, the emission studies were performed with incremental addition of Fe3+ metal ion, and it was very clear from this study that with the addition of Fe3+ metal ion, there was a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This observation states that the carbon dots is highly selective and sensitive towards Fe3+ metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption response of Neo-CA-CQDs to Fe3+ion
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity towards various cations and their interaction was investigated by performing a UV–Vis Spectroscopic study. The absorption and emission spectra were recorded for Neo-CA-CQDs with and without adding Fe3+ metal ions to study this event. The Neo-CA-CQDs showed an absorption maximum at 205 nm region, while with added Fe3+, it was noted that there was an increase in the intensity of the absorption peak. In addition, when the solution was subject to fluorescence, it was very clear that with the addition of Fe3+ metal ions, there was an abrupt decrease in fluorescence at 450nm. The emission spectra for Neo-CA-CQDs were observed at 450 nm, as shown in Fig. 7
a.

All the physiological and biological processes are governed by a significant factor known as pH. Therefore, it is crucial to examine the effect of carbon dots on their fluorescence properties. The pH studies depict that not much change was observed under acidic (pH=4) and neutral (pH=7); however, a slight decrease in emission intensity at pH 9.2 can be seen in Fig. 7 b. This indicates that the synthesized carbon dots are highly stable in acidic, basic and neutral media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of Neo-CA-CQDs for Fe3+ metal ion
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing performance of Neo-CA-CQDs was investigated for various metal ions using DDW. The stock solution of Neo-CA-CQDs was prepared, and all studies were performed at room temperature using DDW. The 10–3 M stock solution of Fe3+ ion was prepared for sensing experiments. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded for Neo-CA-CQDs. The most plausible binding mode for sensing Fe3+ metal ions to the synthesized carbon dots is binding with the COO- and -OH group of Neo-CA-CQDs. It was observed that when Fe3+ metal ions bind to the carbon dots, a quenching of fluorescence is observed. This quenching phenomenon can be due to the photoinduced electron transfer mechanism, commonly observed in most carbon dots sensing applications (Fig. 8 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching efficiency and competitive binding of Neo-CA-CQDs for Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para:
The quenching efficiency(η) for the synthesized Neo-CA-CQDs was calculated for all metal ions by using equation [(I0−I)/I0] × 100 %, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of the various metal ions, such as Fe3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, and Fe2+. The studies revealed that the initial emission intensity for Neo-CA-CQDs showed an abrupt decrease upon adding Fe3+ metal ions. However, this behavior was not observed for other metal cations. Through statistical calculation, the quenching efficiency was found to be 95.28 % (Fig. 9
a). The photostability study for the synthesized carbon dots was performed over 24 h. The photostability investigation observed that as the time period increased, there was no change in fluorescence intensity, as represented in Figure S3.

An interference study was performed to study the effect of other cations on the carbon dots.In this diffrrent cations such as Fe3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Al3+,Pb2+, Zn2+,Mn2+,Cu2+,Co2+,Ni2+,Sn2+,Hg2+, and Fe2+, were added to the solution of Neo-CA-CQDs in presence of Fe3+ metal ion. From Fig. 9 a, it was observed that in the presence of Fe3+ ion, the quenching efficiency was 95.28 %; however, the Fig. 9 b pictogram indicates that when Fe3+ metal ion was added to the solution containing the carbon dots with other interfering cations, there, was an abrupt decrease in the fluorescence intensity. The red bar indicates the carbon dots with metal ions indicates no change in fluorescence intensity, while the green bar indicates CQDs +Fe3++ metal ions, indicating the quenching of fluorescence. This signifies that fluorescence intensity decreases only with the addition of Fe3+ metal ions; however, other metal ions do not affect the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stern-Volmer plot and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The Stern Volmer plot is used to study the interaction dynamics for both the fluorescent material and the quencher. The plot is the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I0/I) and the quencher concentration function [Q]. The following Eq. (1) is used in calculating the Stern−Volmer constant [34]
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
Where I0/I corresponds to fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Fe3+ metal ion, Ksv is the quenching constant. The Stern−Volmer plot indicates the linearity for the Neo-CA-CQDs with Fe3+ metal ion with a lower concentration of 100μM. The positive curvature in the Stern−Volmer plot indicates intersystem crossing, and the charge transfer complex exhibits static quenching. The observed Ksv value for Fe3+ metal ion is 7.6 × 10–6M Hence, it is predicted that the carbon dots showed excellent quenching efficiency when Fe3+ metal ion was added (Fig. 10
b).

The detection limit for the Neo-CA-CQDs was calculated by a statistical approach using equation
(2)
LOD = 3
σ
/ m
where σ is the standard deviation of blank and m is the slope of the method [35]. To evaluate the detection limit, fluorescence titration was performed for the synthesized carbon dots by adding a Fe3+ metal ion. The graph was plotted with metal ion concentration on the X-axis against fluorescence intensity on the Y-axis. When Fe3+ metal ion is added from 0 to 100 μM, there is a decrease in fluorescence intensity in a linear manner Fig. 10 a. The observed limit of detection for the Neo-CA-CQDs is 6.09 μM.

The photostability study was performed for Neo-CA-CQDs in order to examine the stability of the carbon dots over period of time. In this study it can be concluded that there was no significant change in the fluorescence emission intensity upon continuous irradiation of UV light (365 nm) for 30 days. The following result in Figure S3 suggest that the Neo-CA-CQDs has excellent photostability under 365 nm UV light which further confirms that the compound can be utilized for sensing application. In addition to photostability study, time rate study was performed with addition of Fe3+ metal ion. In this study it was observed that when Fe3+ ion was added to Neo-CA-CQDs and kept for 1 h the fluorescence emission intensity decrease which clearly suggest that the Neo-CA-CQDs shows interaction with Fe3+ metal ion (Figure S3 b).

The average lifetime was calculated by fitting a triexponential decay function. The obtained average lifetime for the Neo-CA-CQDs was 2.99 ns which decreases to 2.62 ns with the addition of Fe3+ metal ion. The decrease in lifetime is mainly due to transfer of excited electron from Neo-CA-CQDs to vacant d-orbital of Fe3+ metal ion. This results in quenching of fluorescence via photoinduced electron transfer (Fig. 11 ).

A mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) was used to assess the cytotoxicity of Neo-CA-CQDs. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM), supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum and 0.1 % antibiotic-antimycotic solution for 3–4 passages at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 atmosphere in the incubator (Nuve EC 160, Turkey). At 85 % confluency, the cells were harvested using 0.25 % trypsin and seeded into 24-walled culture plates. The plate was incubated for 24 h before exposure.

The Neo-CA-CQDs were suspended in MilliQ water and filter sterilized using a syringe filter of 0.22µm pore size. The Neo-CA-CQDs stock concentration of 30 mg/ml was serially diluted using DMEM media to obtain 4 desired concentrations of 25 µg/ml, 50 µg/ml, 75 µg/ml, and 100 µg/ml. The different concentrations were added in triplicates, and the cells were subjected to Neo-CA-CQDs for 24 and 48 h. The cytotoxicity of Neo-CA-CQDs was analyzed via colorimetric MTT assay. Similarly, the cell morphological analysis was carried out using an inverted bright field microscope (Evos XL core, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) (See supporting information, Figure S5).

The MTT assay showed a viability shift post 24-hour exposure, wherein the viability was gradually reduced over increasing doses of Neo-CA-CQDs. However, post-48 h of exposure, the cell viability reduction was insignificant compared to 24 h. Figure S6-Figure S7. A slight increase in cell numbers was observed. This is generally observed in routine cytotoxic tests, where the cells try to recover from the shock of toxin exposure. This may have occurred as the effects of Neo-CA-CQDs were ameliorated over time. Similarly, it could also signify the ineffectiveness of the Neo-CA-CQDs on the cell growth dynamics. The cell viability trend for Neo-CA-CQDs over time period of 24 h and 48 h are demonstrated in Table S1.

Similarly the cytotoxicity study was performed for human cervical cancer cell line (HeLa) cells. The detailed methodology is explained in supporting information. The MTT assay was performed for Neo-CA-CQDs which showed a change in viability post 24 h exposure, wherein the viability seems to reduce gradually over increasing doses of Neo-CA-CQDs. There was a significant reduction in viability for the highest exposure concentration. However, post 48 h of exposure, the trend of reduction in cell viability was only seen in higher exposure concentration of 75 µg/ml and 100 µg/ml. There was an increase in cell number observed for 25 µg/ml and 50 µg/ml exposure concentration. This may signify the ineffectiveness of the Neo-CA-CQDs on the cell growth dynamics at lower concentrations. The results are depicted in Figure S9 and Figure S10. The percent viability study revealed that at 24 h and 48 h the contral cell line shows 100 % viability while as the concetration increases the cell viability also decreseas. Table S2.

In summary, neomycin and citric acid synthesized Neo-CA-CQDs by hydrothermal method. The synthesized Neo-CA-CQDs were successfully characterized by FTIR spectroscopy, 13C NMR spectroscopy, UV–Vis, PL, HR-TEM, XPS, DLS, Zeta potential and Powder X-ray Diffraction (PXRD). The fluorescence quantum yield for Neo-CA-CQDs was 29.3 %. The average size of Neo-CA-CQDs was analyzed by HR-TEM, and it was found to be 3 to 5.7 nm, with good solution dispersal. The as-prepared Neo-CA-CQDs, without purification, have been used as a sensor for Fe3+ions. The Neo-CA-CQDs have excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ metal ions in the presence of other competing metal ions. Further, the Neo-CA-CQDs were also investigated for their photostability, pH, and ionic strength. This clearly indicated no change in fluorescence intensity over time along with pH and no change in fluorescence over the change in ionic strength using NaCl. This clearly states that the Neo-CA-CQDs can be well utilized over changes in various parameters such as time, pH, and ionic strength, which signifies wide application in various scientific fields. In addition, a toxicity study was performed for Neo-CA-CQDs on mouse fibroblast cell line L929 and HeLa cell. Thus, from the above studies, it can be concluded that functionalized neomycin with citric acid has a more significant advantage and will solve global environmental pollution issues. It is also an excellent advantage in the field of biomedical application.

Pritesh P. Khobrekar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Geeta A. Zalmi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Antara P. Raiturker: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Ratan W. Jadhav: Writing – review & editing. Anasuya Ganguly: Supervision. Avelyno D'Costa: Supervision. Sandesh T. Bugde: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Sheshanath V. Bhosale: Supervision.

We acknowledge DST-FIST,UGC and SCS, Goa University for the instrumentation facility. We acknowledge IIT Bombay SAIF for providing HR-TEM facility, IIT Kanpur for XPS facility and IISc Bangalore for time resolved fluorescence.
